narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on factors like academic history, test scores, and background to forecast bar exam success, estimated an 82.8% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed in their legal careers. For you, a Black male with a lower-middle family income background, the model's decision hinged most strongly on your undergraduate academic performance, particularly your rankings in school deciles during your first and third years, which both placed you in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's mid-range standing around the fifth or sixth decile. These low decile rankings were the top two factors pushing the prediction heavily toward failure, signaling to the model a consistent struggle with coursework that often correlates with bar exam challenges. Your LSAT score of 28, well under the average of about 37, ranked third in influence and further nudged the outlook toward not passing, as stronger scores typically bolster success odds. While your choice to pursue full-time studies aligns with most applicants, it couldn't offset these core academic signals. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker early and later undergrad rankings alongside a below-average LSAT and GPA of 2.1 versus the usual 3.2, painting a picture of higher risk despite your determination. The model doesn't weigh personal background like race or gender heavily here, but it does highlight how these academic metrics stand out as barriers in your case. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many in similar circumstances have overcome such starts. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for at least the average range, bolstering your undergrad record through post-bac courses or targeted tutoring to improve those decile equivalents, and gathering strong recommendation letters that speak to your growth. Consider part-time programs or bridge courses designed for students building stronger academic foundations, and reapply after demonstrating these improvements—schools value persistence and evidence of upward trends. With deliberate steps, you can shift your profile toward the successful passers the model favors.
